data.title: Centrus Energy: Sell Nuclear Stocks Before The Rally Wears Thin
data.description:
I have been a long-time optimist on nuclear power. As far back as 2021, I was writing about how it seemed likely that the United States and the world as a whole would need more nuclear power to address future electricity demand.

There's little dispute that humanity will ultimately keep pushing toward more sustainable green power coming from sources such as solar and wind. Until recently, many people had written off nuclear as a dangerous expensive alternative to wind and solar.

However, wind and solar have a serious issue of their own due to their intermittent power generation and the lack of installed battery storage capacity. There would also need to be large investments in grid modernization and expansion to move electricity from sunny deserts and sparse windy regions over to the big cities where demand is concentrated.

I'd assume there will be significant improvements in both the power transmission system and in battery storage technologies to harness intermittent power sources more efficiently. But these are likely to play out over a scale of many years or even decades. In the near-term, however, heavy reliance on intermittent power generation sources can lead to significant consequences. For example, some experts have suggested that the recent nationwide blackout in Spain may have been triggered in part by increasing integration of solar power into that nation's overall grid.

Given the current limitations in place, it seems likely that a combination of nuclear and natural gas should work as bridge fuels over the next couple of decades as we move toward affordable and reliable deployment of other green power sources. While natural gas and nuclear aren't perfect, they have significantly fewer environmental drawbacks as opposed to burning other fuel sources such as coal or heavy oil for electricity.

Nuclear Is Once Again A Hot Investment Theme

Historically, I've invested in companies such as BWX Technologies (BWXT) to ride this potential nuclear renaissance. I look at the sector as one where long-term growth is likely to occur at a rate well above GDP. I am all aboard the nuclear bandwagon as it pertains to the technology.

And within the nuclear stocks category, sentiment was bound to improve as the Fukushima nuclear accident starts to fade from peoples' immediate memory. On the plus side, the widescale adoption of power-hungry AI data centers could drive substantial demand for electricity. More broadly, the electrification of many things in society, such as transportation vehicles, should serve as a tailwind for electricity demand.

However, I believe optimism has currently run well ahead of where it should be at this point. Just look at price action on many nuclear-themed stocks and ETFs over the past month:

In May alone, today's subject, Centrus Energy Corp. (NYSE:LEU) is up 74%. Some of the small reactor stocks are up as much as 100%. And the broad industry ETFs such as Global X Uranium (URA) are up more than 20% over the past 30 days.

There is widespread optimism across the nuclear power industry. Be it uranium mining, uranium processing, or firms in fields such as small nuclear reactor development, traders are excited.

The most recent rally comes, in large part, because the Trump Administration has signed executive orders which intend to bring about a fourfold increase in the size of the United States' nuclear power generation fleet by 2050. In addition, analysts expect that we'll see more nuclear power used in defense applications such as for powering Navy vessels. And on the commercial side, we see utilities with nuclear power generation capability teaming up with technology firms to power AI growth.

Combine all these factors, and we've seen the nuclear power stocks become white hot in 2025.

However, we're arguably reaching a point where the hype has gotten well ahead of the underlying fundamentals. Increasingly, it seems that short-term traders are pushing these stocks up as much as double digits on a day-to-day basis. But, in reality, it's probably going to take many years for these executive orders and new nuclear reactor technologies to start to generate meaningful revenues or profits.

Centrus Energy: Exciting Possibilities, But Upside Already Priced In

Centrus Energy offers a great example where traders, in their recent enthusiasm, have already priced in gains from future developments which will take years to play out assuming they happen at all.

LEU stock had been trading for around $40/share for the majority of the past three years. Then, last fall, it blasted off to $100. After sliding back to $50 earlier this year, LEU stock has now doubled in less than two months.

Are the company's prospects at some sort of intermediate-term turning point?

According to analysts, no, they are not. Analysts see Centrus Energy's earnings falling in both 2025 and 2026 before starting to increase in 2027. Additionally, the company's revenues are expected to outright decline this year and rise at a fairly modest pace in 2026 and 2027 despite the sizzling action in the company's stock price:

That's because this new wave of nuclear innovation will take a long time to actually generate meaningful revenues for a company such as Centrus.

Investors are particularly excited about the potential for high-assay low-enriched uranium ("HALEU"), where Centrus has a significant first mover advantage. Centrus should have a dominant position in U.S. HALEU capacity for many years, though there are seemingly viable competitors in Europe and Russia.

More broadly, HALEU could be much more efficient than traditional uranium fuel, and thus gain large market share with the new generation of small modular reactor designs. If Centrus can grab a significant chunk of that market, earnings per share should rise over time.

However, keep in mind that the Biden Administration provided substantial resources and public support for the creation of the HALEU supply chain. This is a process that has already been in the works for years; nothing magically changed a month ago.

Trump's executive orders on nuclear power should bolster the industry's outlook over the long-term. However, there's been bipartisan interest in nuclear for several years now. And some actions of the new administration, such as the temporary firing of many workers at the National Nuclear Security Administration as part of the Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) reductions, likely created confusion and uncertainty in the regulatory environment for nuclear projects.

As for the small modular reactors, they still need to go through permitting before we can realistically start to make projections around commercial deployment at scale. Expert commentary that I've seen suggests that the mid-2030s would be a reasonable timeline for getting these into popular commercial adoption. Centrus may have the pole position on HALEU technology today, but a decade is a long time to wait before shareholders will likely see material upside from this. In the meantime, Centrus' profits will largely come from existing nuclear fueling technology which is perfectly fine, but perhaps not worthy of a more than 30x forward P/E ratio.

Put another way, I believe that there is a significant duration mismatch between the current trader enthusiasm and the time when fundamentals will catch up to the underlying story.

For example, take a look at the company's price-to-sales ratio over time. It surged from less than 1x five years ago to almost 4.5x now:

I'm not denying that the growth is coming. I firmly believe there will be a lot more nuclear power capacity both in the United States and overseas over, say, the next two decades. But more revenues and earnings 10 to 20 years from now will not necessarily support the price of LEU stock over the next week, month, or year.

LEU Stock Verdict

Bulls are pointing to huge growth in the nuclear industry over time. I fully agree that there's a reasonable probability of that outcome occurring.

But there are still many hurdles to clear before nuclear power generation really takes off. The small modular reactors, in particular, still have a long way to go in terms of obtaining regulatory approval and being proven to have a profitable business model at scale.

We can and should start to account for future cash flows and profits that Centrus will eventually earn from the ongoing nuclear renaissance.

That said, I expect traders may get frustrated when it sinks in that more nuclear reactors being built five or ten years from now is not going to affect Centrus' earnings anytime soon.

It's also worth asking how much new information has really happened over the past month compared to when LEU stock was trading at $60 back in April. It was a fairly consensus view that the Trump Administration had a favorable view toward nuclear power and also that AI advancements were driving more demand for electricity.

The executive orders are a nice positive development. But they're not something that came completely out of left field. I see them as a fairly thin justification for the doubling we've seen in LEU shares along with that of the small nuclear reactor names in recent weeks.

I'll conclude this piece with a remark from the most recent Constellation Energy (CEG) conference call. Constellation is one of the leaders in nuclear power generation. Constellation CEO Joesph Dominguez commented that:

"While we think there are some out there, who have overstated the amount of new [nuclear energy] demand for their own reasons, we're confident that the demand can be met, and that the markets will respond with demand response and new generation as needed [...]

While the demand is strong, we have to be a bit cautious here that there are some claims that are a bit outsized in terms of the extent or amount of that demand, claims that are difficult to substantiate either mathematically or logically [...]

In fact, we've done the math, and if NVIDIA were able to double its output and every single chip went to ERCOT, it still wouldn't be enough chips to support some of the load forecasts in ERCOT. There's been a history of over-forecasting. We've seen this before with electric vehicles. We've seen it before with the Internet. And utilities have historically overestimated their load forecasts."

That seems like a reasonable middle ground. There is a lot of new demand. The market will be able to meet that demand with new supply as needed. However, there are some folks that may be taking too rosy a view of just how much new supply is needed and how quickly it will come on-line.

I'm a long-term nuclear bull. But when prices double in a month, the hype starts to get ahead of the reality. As such, I'd be a seller on LEU shares here and look for a significant pullback before reentering a position.

data.title: The Hard Asset Revolution And Why It Will Spin Off Fortunes
data.description:
By Mike Larson

In this episode of the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast, Larry McDonald, founder of The Bear Traps Report and the former Lehman Brothers VP who predicted the subprime crisis, reveals why we're heading into an unprecedented environment for hard assets that will reshape global markets - and create massive wealth for those positioned correctly.

Transcript

Mike Larson: When markets speak, the most successful investors listen. But how can you do the same? How can you figure out which market moves to listen to, which to ignore, and most importantly, take advantage of the signals the important ones are sending out.

Joining me to share his insights is another all new guest on the MoneyShow MoneyMasters Podcast. He has a long and distinguished history of identifying big macro trends and warning of some of the biggest market pitfalls of the last couple of decades. He is Larry McDonald, Founder of The Bear Traps Report and author of the 2024 book, How to Listen When Markets Speak. Larry, welcome to the podcast.

Well, Larry, I do appreciate you taking some time out to chat, especially since you're on the proverbial road here. I haven't had you on the podcast before. So I was wondering briefly, if you could just talk a little bit about your background and The Bear Traps Report. I mean, as a trader I know what a bear trap is, but I'm curious why you went with that name for your publication and your research platform.

Larry McDonald: Oh, thank you, Mike, and thanks so much to the MoneyShow. What a platform you guys have built over the last decade.

ML: Appreciate that.

LM: Thank you very much for that. So our first book was published in 12 languages. It's a New York Times bestseller. And I quickly learned - it's a little inside baseball joke - but you make about 10 times more in the speaking tour than you do writing the book.

And so we did about 140 speeches, 16 countries around the world, working with the big Fidelitys, the Januses, all the big funds.

ML: Yeah.

LM: And then what we did was we developed kind of a Bloomberg chat with the people that we met. And so we met some incredibly talented people, and I'm so grateful to our contributors. So what we host at The Bear Traps Report every day globally is kind of like a live ideas dinner...

ML: Yeah.

LM: ... where you have a conversation with the best hedge fund managers in the world. Most of it's anonymous, but because we've been to London maybe 8 times in the last decade, Toronto, Montreal, LA, we built - we've just built some incredible relationships. And that's how we produced our second book, How to Listen When Markets Speak.

This book is - you know we sit down with David Tepper, we sit down with Charlie Munger, we sit down with David Einhorn, and some of the greats, Niall Ferguson. And it was all about those deposits and those relationships that we built over 15 years coming off the first book. And that is what we want to bring to the MoneyShow audience in July.

ML: Well, I'm thrilled. We're extremely pleased to have you there in Vegas. And I do want to talk about the book a little bit. I mean, obviously, you have a goal there with this new book. You want to teach investors something. What are some of those goals? What are investors going to get out of this book from you?

LM: Well, it’s - if you know, there's no "I" in teams, so it's about kind of learning and it's about paying attention. And so I think [what] people need to do in watching our event this summer is really kind of build mentors. You want to build great mentors and develop relationships with people around the world that you can trust, and gain trust. It's all about asking and making deposits, making deposits and then getting the withdrawals by working with some great mentors - and that's what we've done.

But in terms of investment ideas, the one thing that we're getting from the chat - and so, once again, we host a live conversation on the Bloomberg Terminal and the Discord with hedge funds, mutual funds, and pension funds in more than 20 countries.

ML: Yeah.

LM: The one thing, Mike, that I'm hearing this last eight weeks is that for the first time, you've got - your stocks have been down, your bonds have been down, and your dollar is down, and those global allocators are really looking at the United States in a different way.

And what's fascinating is we've had multiple big clients come in and ask us about Treasury issuance, about what's called the TGA - the Treasury General Account - how much money is in there. We've got, right around the time of our event in July, we've got a real kind of showdown on the debt ceiling. This is going to be incredible for July and August.

And so, what institutional investors want to know is, the United States is becoming just a tiny, tiny, tiny little bit more like an emerging market country, in the sense that we have a lot of debt issuance to catch up on. In other words, right now you're at the debt ceiling, so there's no new debt issuance. But after our conference in July, from like late July to the end of the year, Mike, they have to catch up - and this is going to be pretty incredible.

That's why you're starting to see things like the SLR being floated by Scott Bessent. And it has huge impact on equity investors, because - so from the bottom lines, from what I can hear, clients have an interest in kind of a new product where we're looking at Treasury issuance, we're looking at the budget, we're looking at tax receipts, we're looking at the TGA, the RRP.

And the bottom line is, people want to figure out how much debt is coming to the market for sale in the second half of the year and how are we going to raise that money? Is it going to be from institutional investors? Is it going to be from the banks through the SLR? This is what we have to get down, because it's going to have a huge impact on equities.

ML: Well, interest rates have to be the real story here, right? I mean, you've got things like the announcements out of Japan, they're going to kind of tweak how they're issuing debt to maybe help at the long end of the curve there. Obviously, you mentioned some of the things that could happen with banking regulation and so on here in the U.S. How do you see that unfolding as the year goes on?

LM: Well, one of the points we make in the book, How to Listen When Markets Speak, is around financial repression. And so, the only way you get out of a $37-38 trillion debt hole - there's two ways. There's default, or there's keeping interest rates or massaging interest rates below the rate of inflation.

And so, one of the points we make in the book is around things like this SLR move, where they're going to try to force different types of investors to own more Treasuries. They may force our trading partners to own more Treasuries, and they want to get rates below the rate of inflation.

And what that means is that 2010-2020 portfolio doesn't work anymore, because that was a disinflationary regime portfolio. Now we're looking for a regime shift where we're looking in toward financial repression. And in a financial repression world, you need a portfolio that has a whole different construction.

ML: And let's talk about that a little bit. I mean, obviously, so far this year, despite the original kind of Trump trade, which was "Buy America", so far this year, it's essentially at times been "Sell America" - U.S. bonds, U.S. dollar, U.S. stocks relative to foreign stocks. How do you see that all unfolding?

LM: Well, that's it. It's the 2010-2020 portfolio was U.S.-centric growth stocks, real crowded trade. And the bigger it got, the more passive funds own the same things. Now this 2025-35 portfolio, much more international stocks, much more value, much more emerging markets - we love Brazil. But it's also companies that own hard assets, companies that own things - where, if you're in an elevated bond yield regime with higher sustained inflation, we want to be in a portfolio of companies that own hard assets.

ML: Got it. And I mean, I would assume that that would include things that are - that when people think of hard assets, they think of gold, they think of mining companies, things like that. Is that what you're looking at, or what else are you kind of putting in that bucket?

LM: Yeah. So your Rio Tintos, your PHPs, your Exxons, your natural gas companies, that's not a hard asset, but natural gas is a huge AI winner. And just look at the NUKZ ETF. We've been recommending that in trade alerts over the last two months. And it's a portfolio of what we call independent power producers and companies in the nuclear uranium space.

And so, that type of portfolio is outperforming the semiconductors, which is the Street’s consensus AI trade by over 50% over the last six, nine months. That's the type of portfolio construction that's going to work the next 10 years.

ML: Got it.

Earlier, in an interview you had, I think, it was on Fox Business with Maria Bartiromo, you talked about sort of the one of the scarier scenarios was the 4,000-4,000 - S&P 4,000, gold 4,000. Do you think something like that is likely possible? How worried should general stock investors be about this environment we have?

LM: Well, the indexes are, if you just look at NVIDIA (NVDA), it's $3.3 trillion. And the top three stocks in the NUKZ are less than $300 billion. So, NVIDIA - one stock that's in the S&P - is so large compared to all the other players in AI.

And so, the point that we're kind of making is that the index construction of the S&P 500s are passive, has gotten so distorted that yeah, you could see - through a softening in the economy, through elevated inflation - you could see a move. But you probably are right now in a 1968-1981 regime, where the stock market doesn't go anywhere, but within the market there are sectors that really shine.

ML: Got it. Got it. And in some of those international markets, obviously, you mentioned Brazil was one. Are there others particularly that look promising to you, either regions or single countries?

LM: Well, Indonesia has been really hammered. There's been some political stress here. That's really speculative. Peru and Chile, you've got very large copper exposure there. They should be just wonderful places to be in the next 10 years.

We are facing a copper shortage of unprecedented proportions. Just look at the Ukraine rebuild. Look at the U.S. power grid, the next $2 trillion rebuild for the U.S. power grid to support AI - $2 trillion, a lot of copper there. Robotics - we're going to need 2 million robots. If you do the math, that's a lot of copper. And so the LA rebuild, right, Los Angeles - it's going to be slow, but it has to be rebuilt.

So, Ukraine, L.A., robotics, the U.S. power grid - we are coming into a spectacular bull market for copper in countries that are big copper producers, your Chiles and your Perus. Those types of ETFs really will do quite well.

ML: Got it. What about your thoughts on Bitcoin? You had a post recently on X talking about how it seemed to be maturing, that part of the market was maturing. And I'm kind of interested in whether we're finally breaking that Bitcoin is just tech on steroids. It's more like a digital gold-type asset. I was wondering about your thoughts there.

LM: Yeah, what's annoyed me about Bitcoin - and we're big Bitcoin supporters in the book. But what annoys me is that like in August, for example, you had the Bank of Japan, some stress there - carry trade is blowing up. And in three days, because of liquidity, Bitcoin is off 35% - in literally three or four days. That's not a store of value. That's an asset that hurts families, it hurts young kids, the "puke it out". Those three drawdowns of 80%, 90% since 2017 have hurt a lot of people.

But what's encouraging is, because we've gone into this new regime of bonds down, stocks down, dollar down a number of days in the last 30 days - we probably had like 12 days in the last 45, 50 days where stocks are down, bonds are down, and the dollar is down. And it's interesting, and I'm really happy for Bitcoin - people that own it, because it's interesting that on those types of days, Bitcoin is doing just fine, and which is the total regime shift. Yeah. So I think something's going on there with Bitcoin.

ML: Okay, good. Perfect. Thoughts on the economy, that's one that we kind of didn't really cover. I mean, do you think recession worries are overblown, understated? What are your thoughts there?

LM: Well, right now, the one trade that's worked over the last two, three years is that we've had periods where you go from hard landing expectations are high, like think of fourth quarter of 2023, right, or ‘22, ‘23 - I should say the first second quarter of ‘23 with the banks blowing up in late ‘22.

So you've gone through periods where that hard landing expectation is high, and that was - go back to April 7th of this year, the perception of a hard landing was very high. Now we're back to the perception of a soft landing is high. And so, the key to the last four years is fading that. Like, in other words, when you see that soft landing projection from the Street coming out, you want to be lightening stocks raising cash. And then when those expectations of a hard landing rise, that's when you want to be putting more money to work.

But yeah, bottom line is, are we going to have some type of recession here in the next like six, nine months? We're seeing - yes, we're seeing big moves in the - big, big, big trend shifts in volatility in what we call "buy now, pay later" names.

So your subprime lenders, your Ally Financials, your credit acceptance companies, your Affirm, UPST. You've got Upstart, you've got a lot of these subprime lenders that are showing real weakness relative to the big financials. So to me, that's something that it reminds me of late fourth quarter 2007.

ML: Got it. Okay. The time we have left, I guess one macro topic we haven't covered is really central banks and particularly the Fed, sort of what your thoughts are there? I mean, they've been sitting on their hands for a while after those cuts last year. What are your thoughts on the outlook?

LM: Well, we're going to have a new Fed governor this time next year. Powell, his term is over and Trump is going to appoint probably a more market-friendly Fed governor. And then, at the end of the day, the Fed is playing tough guy. They're creating some stresses on the - on, like I said, on the subprime side.

Yeah, so does the Fed cut rates a lot more than expectations from here for the next six, nine months? Yes. Like in other words, the probability that the Fed can stay this hawkish - I mean, higher for longer creates a lot of problems in commercial real estate, it creates a lot of problems for the bottom 60% of consumers, home buyers. I mean, home affordability is a complete joke. It's a disaster right now for home affordability.

So, is the Fed politically pushed into more cuts? Yeah, we're going to have a lot more dovish Fed in the next six, nine months than we have right now.

ML: Got it. I guess my - one of my last questions would be any kind of out-of-consensus ideas, trends, or something we haven't talked about yet but that intrigue you at this point in time?

LM: Well, the one thing is that we're seeing some fly in some of the institutional chat, they're buying platinum, palladium. So if you think of platinum, palladium - the entire market cap is $350 billion, right? It's tiny relative to gold, close to 18 trillion, silver, maybe 1.6 trillion.

So platinum and palladium, if you go into that hard asset regime, your Sibanye-Stillwaters, your Impala Platinums, these stocks are breaking out. We've been recommending them the last six months. It's the unloved kind of, what we call the bombed-out village, right? It reminds me of Cameco, your uranium names in late 2020-21. We're talking about a bombed-out village. We're talking about a multiple year bull, sorry, bear market where lots - every rally was a fade and you had lower highs and lower lows.

And now you're coming out of that, you're coming into the churn for platinum, palladium for a whole bunch of different reasons. You've got buyers in China, you've got a big move toward hybrids, and you have a world that needs hard assets now.

So yeah, do I want to own platinum and palladium relative to Bitcoin right now? If I had a $1 million, do I put it in platinum and palladium or Bitcoin at the highs? Platinum and palladium.

ML: Okay. Well, Larry, I really do appreciate you taking some time out to chat here, especially on the road.

Larry, congratulations on the book. Thanks for joining me, and look forward to seeing you in July.

LM: Thank you, Mike.

Originally Published on MoneyShow.com

data.title: Trump And Bessent Do A 180-Degree Turn And That Is A Very Bullish Factor For The Stock Market
data.description:
Last December 1, weeks after President Donald Trump's election victory and practically two months before he took office, I published a counterintuitive analysis where I explained that markets love stimulus, such as a high fiscal deficit, and hate the opposite, when a government tightens its belt and reduces spending.

Thus, given that U.S. public finances maintain an unsustainable trajectory due to its very high deficit, a level not seen outside times of economic recessions and wars (sic), if indeed the Trump administration, with its Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent at the helm, were to implement a more or less hard line to save the US economy in the long term, that could be negative for the markets because the main stimulus that makes the stock markets rise today, the fiscal deficit, spending, or the torrent of money that the State throws on the economy, would disappear.

I talked about this a great deal during 2022 and 2023, to explain how, even with a Federal Reserve raising interest rates at the fastest pace in over 40 years, we did not go into recession because the huge fiscal deficit prevented it.

In other words, just as we all know that a Fed asset purchase program (QE) is a strong stimulus for the markets, government spending like there is no tomorrow (record fiscal deficits) is a stimulus of the same caliber.

Well, finally Trump and Bessent, as soon as they took office in January of this year, embraced their particular version of belt-tightening and started chanting that their mission was to fight for the people in the street (Main Street) and not for the markets (Wall Street). Even Trump went so far as to say, and this is comical, that he didn't care what happened in the markets, as his vision was 100 years out.

"The market and the economy have become addicted to excessive government spending and we are going to go through a detox period” (Scott Bessent, US Treasury Secretary, March 7, 2025)

"There could be a little bit of [economic] disruption because we are building a strong country, where we can't be looking at [just] what happens with the stock market. If you look at China, they have a 100-year outlook and we go quarter by quarter and we can't manage that way." (Donald Trump, March 9, 2025)

Thus, with all that rhetoric, Trump and Bessent managed to create a stock market crash, from the highs at the end of February to the lows of April.

Their plan was to announce the application of tariffs globally, both to allied and non-allied countries, knowing that such an announcement would bring turbulence and a cooling of economic expectations., which they hoped would force the Federal Reserve to lower official interest rates and thus begin to fast-track deficit reduction through better refinancing of the country's massive debt.

But the move backfired.

Although at the beginning of the “Trump crash” long-term interest rates began to fall and even went below 4% momentarily (10-year bond yields), this was only the initial reaction because bond yields then rebounded strongly and various elements of the debt and credit markets began to break down, setting off alarm bells that if they continued on this path, the economy and markets would implode.

That is why just a few days after provoking a stock market crash, Donald Trump announced the postponement of most of the announced tariffs, provoking an anticrac, or movement just as energetic as the fall, but in the opposite direction.

This is how we have lived a round trip, where Trump tried to reduce the deficit by the fast track creating an economic-stock market scare, but where the market showed its teeth and, so that the same thing would not happen to him as happened to the very short-lived Prime Minister of the United Kingdom, Liz Truss, who had to resign after a month and a half in power for also having provoked a strong crisis in the debt market of her country, Trump decided to change his speech and forget about reducing the deficit in the short term.

In just a month and a half, Trump and Bessent have gone from saying that they were the champions of "the people on the street" and that they didn't care about the reaction on Wall Street, to showing themselves as docile "good guys" incapable of saying anything that would upset the markets.

Thus, as of a few days ago, Scott Bessent is no longer talking about making Wall Street suffer for the sake of Main Street by tightening the belt of public spending, but that the important thing is to make the economy grow faster than the debt and that this would be enough to reduce the debt ratio.

"We can grow the economy and control debt at the same time. The important thing is to grow the economy faster than the debt. If we change the trajectory of economic growth in this country, then our finances will stabilize and we can get out of this problem." (Scott Bessent May 23, 2025)

This, and other similar statements of these days, could seem like any of the many phrases that a politician says, but they are a 180-degree turn from what he said just a month and a half ago.

Where before it was necessary to go through a moment of economic cooling and short-term turbulence to fix the problems, now the message is that the only important thing is to grow and that the debt will be fixed little by little.

In my view, this is the most relevant thing that has happened in the markets since the Trump crash. This complete shift in the mindset of the Trump administration is a big bullish factor for the stock market and other risk markets.

This is so because Trump and Bessent are the fiscal authority and if the fiscal authority, which today is the main factor influencing the markets, points out that they are not going to be reducing debt in nominal terms and that the important thing is to grow more, it is like getting the green light to go back to thinking that the probabilities are bullish in the medium term in risk assets.

Therefore, if last December I warned that what could be very positive for the economy in the long term (belt-tightening) would be strongly negative for the markets, now it is as if the opposite were true: “What is bad for the economy in the long term is usually good for the stock market”.

The U.S. government panicked with its own crash and is now banking everything on growing strongly without reducing public spending stimulus. And that is something the markets love because stocks tend to rise when there is stimulus, whether fiscal or monetary.

In fact, another element that confirms this Copernican turn in the mentality of Trump and Bessent is that just yesterday the tycoon Elon Musk announced his departure from the government. Recall that with his entrance DOGE, the Department of Government Efficiency, was created and was one of the main symbols of a government that was going to tighten its spending belt.

In the end, although DOGE succeeded in reducing some spending items, its efforts were miniscule in the tsunami of federal government spending.

That Elon Musk is now leaving is just one more sign of the new “economic growth at any cost” mentality embraced by the President and his Treasury Secretary.

All in all, (i) given Trump's turnaround in his trade war and that now every time he announces a tariff hike he ends up postponing it soon after, as happened this week with the theoretical hike he was going to impose on imports from the European Union, and (ii) that the economy is still in an expansion or growth phase and (iii) that the mindset of the US government now is to grow economically at all costs, even if that means increasing debt in nominal terms, I think the macro factors are very positive going forward.

We have economic growth coupled with a fiscal authority that encourages growth with rising debt, something that is typically a tailwind for the stock market.

data.title: Cooper Companies: I'm Seeing More Appeal Here
data.description:
In the summer of last year I called The Cooper Companies, Inc. (NASDAQ:COO) still being in an expensive place. This came after the company actively pursued M&A to grow its CooperSurgical business, to thereby creating greater diversification from its main CooperVision business.

With shares trading range bound for quite a few years, while solid organic growth was delivered upon, shares were growing into a premium valuation over time.

Right now, the business has seen adjusted earnings multiples come down to valuation multiples in the high teens, all of which start to look quite compelling here.

A Dual Medical Business

Cooper Companies describes itself as a leading global medical device company. Founded in 1958, the company over time has grown to a nearly $4 billion revenue base, employing over 16,000 workers, with products sold exceeding 100 countries across the world.

The core of the business remains CooperVision, a segment making up about two-thirds of sales, in which the company competes against Alcon Inc. (ALC) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) in a global lens market. This oligopolistic market structure, as well as higher barriers to entering this market, creates a very interesting market segment.

The remainder of the sales are generated within the CooperSurgical business, a medical device and fertility business that focuses especially on women. This segment has seen a boost from a string of deals announced in recent years.

Over the past decade, the company has made solid inroads as a $1.8 billion business in 2015 and has grown to a $4.1 billion revenue base (as expected in 2025). CooperVision has seen sales grow from $1.5 billion to $2.8 billion over this time window, with CooperSurgical seeing revenues quadruple from just over $300 million to over $1.3 billion.

A $40 stock in 2015 rose to the $100 mark in 2021 and ever since has largely traded in a $70-$110 trading range over the past couple of years. Those ranges might sound a bit strange to long-term holders, but this came after a 4-for-1 stock split was performed in 2024.

Expectations Come In

Following years of trading stagnation, while the company has seen modest growth, valuation multiples have gradually come down a bit. Nonetheless, valuation multiples were still demanding by all means, after I reviewed the outlook for the shares in the late summer of last year, following a $100 million deal to acquire obp Surgical. This was just a bolt-on deal, adding about 0.4%, or $14.5 million, to pro forma sales.

With shares trading at $95, Cooper's equity was valued at $19 billion, or nearly $22 billion for the entire business, after factoring in $2.7 billion in net debt. This valued the business at nearly 6 times sales and earnings at 27 times adjusted earnings, still premium multiples.

This made me a bit cautious, for good reasons, as forwarding about three-quarters of a year in time, shares have fallen some 20%, reducing these premiums a bit here.

In December of last year, Cooper reported a more than 8% increase in full year sales to $3.90 billion. The company reported GAAP earnings of $392 million, equal to $1.96 per share. Adjusted earnings came in a lot higher, reported up forty-nine cents to $3.69 per share, with most of the reconciliation due to amortization of intangibles, as well as multiple other small things. Net debt was reported at $2.5 billion, coming down a bit amidst retained earnings, solid cash flows and modest dilution incurred.

The company guided for 2025 sales to advance to $4.08-$4.16 billion, with organic growth seen at 6-8%, although that organic growth is seen a bit higher for the Vision segment. Adjusted earnings are seen around $3.97 per share, plus or minus five cents.

In March, Cooper reported somewhat soft first quarter results, with revenues up just over 3% to $965 million, all while net debt ticked down a bit further to $2.44 billion. Despite the softer first quarter growth numbers, Cooper actually maintained the full year sales guidance, and increased the lower end of the full year earnings guidance by two pennies to $3.94 per share.

By the end of May, Cooper reported second quarter sales up 6% to $1.00 billion, supported by largely equal growth across both segments, with adjusted earnings up 14% to $0.96 per share. Following the sound results, the full year sales guidance has been largely narrowed to $4.11-$4.15 billion, with non-GAAP earnings seen at a midpoint of $4.08 per share. This is quite solid, as the company quantified the anticipated tariff impact at 3% to 2026 earnings, assuming no mitigating actions.

With just over 200 million shares now trading at $75, the market value of the firm has fallen to $15 billion, for an enterprise valuation of around $17.5 billion. Shares fell 5% in response to the second quarter earnings results which frankly comes as a bit surprising to me, as second quarter results look quite alright, with the company even upping the full year earnings guidance.

This results in a more reasonable sales multiple was based on the revised guidance for 2025, with shares trading around 19 times adjusted earnings. This multiple looks a lot more attractive already.

What Now?

The reality is that I am getting a bit more upbeat on shares of Cooper Companies, here. The company displays continued growth as leverage remains very reasonable at 1.9 times, certainly in light of the stable growth still reported by the business. All this makes me quite upbeat, although Alcon recently caused some shadow on the industry, following its soft 2025 guidance, although concerns about this are overblown, as it appears if we look at the second quarter results.

While that is something to keep note of, nonetheless, Alcon has been underperforming its peers a bit as well in all honesty, as the valuation of Cooper now looks quite compelling compared to its peer. There is another potential tailwind, as the recent dollar weakness might boost the dollar in reported results, with nearly half of sales generated abroad.

The combination of solid growth, long-term stagnation (and recently share price declines) in combination with potential strength seen from a soft dollar, is all comforting here. This makes me more upbeat about the shares here, with valuation levels starting to offer real appeal here.

data.title: EIM: Diversified Muni CEF For Monthly Income With Improving Distribution Coverage
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Eaton Vance Municipal Bond Fund (NYSE:EIM) is a closed-end fund providing investors with exposure to a diversified portfolio of municipal bonds. The main attraction is the high level of federally tax-free distributions that come via their muni holdings. For some investors who live in states with income tax, there can be a portion of the distribution that is further tax-free at the state level as well.

The fund utilizes leverage in an effort to boost income generation, though with higher short-term rates, that's been more difficult to do. The 100 basis points of cuts the Fed implemented in late 2024 helped, but with current projections, the number of rate cuts going forward has been coming down. Further, the fund is going to be quite sensitive to long-term rates as well, with the 10-Year Treasury putting downward pressure on the fund more recently.

Along with that downward pressure potentially adding to a more attractive time to consider the fund, the discount on the fund remains somewhat appealing. At least, it remains appealing enough to continue to 'Hold' as it trades around its longer-term average. More importantly, though, we have the latest semi-annual report now available to dive into.

EIM Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.09
  • Discount/Premium: -7.69%
  • Distribution Yield: 6.35%
  • Expense Ratio: 0.97%
  • Leverage: 35.20%
  • Managed Assets: $901.58 million
  • Structure: Perpetual

EIM's investment objective is to “provide current income exempt from federal income tax.”

They intend to achieve this through:

... at least 80% of the Fund’s net assets will be invested in municipal obligations, the interest on which is exempt from federal income tax, including the alternative minimum tax (“AMT”), and that are rated A or better by Moody’s Investors Service, Inc. (“Moody’s”), S&P Global Ratings (“S&P”) or Fitch Ratings (“Fitch”). The foregoing 80% policy may not be changed without shareholder approval. Under normal market conditions, the Fund expects to be fully invested (at least 95% of its net assets) in accordance with its investment objective. The Fund may invest up to 20% of its net assets in municipal obligations rated BBB/Baa or below (or unrated obligations deemed by the Fund’s adviser, Eaton Vance Management (“Eaton Vance”), to be of equivalent quality), provided that not more than 15% of its net assets may be invested in municipal obligations rated below B (or unrated obligations deemed by Eaton Vance to be of equivalent quality) and may invest up to 20% of its net assets in bonds on which the interest is subject to the AMT. When a municipal obligation is split rated (meaning rated in different categories by Moody’s, S&P, or Fitch) the Fund will deem the higher rating to apply.

Performance – Downward Pressure

Since our last update, the fund has seen its total returns in the red. The S&P 500 Index isn't an appropriate benchmark, but it can help to illustrate that it wasn't doing so hot during this period either. This came as the result of added uncertainty around tariffs and an overall stretched market that seemed like it needed a reason for a decline anyway.

Increased pressure from long-term rates more recently has put further downward pressure on EIM as muni bonds are traditionally long-duration assets. The fund has an average effective duration of 7.47 years, but this is impacted further by leverage—with a leverage-adjusted duration of 11.31 years. Due to rising long-term risk-free Treasury rates overall, munis have been pressured, and historical results are now nothing to boast about.

EIM has suffered further due to its leverage, amplifying the downside moves. Where EIM is a bit more attractive is when you consider the discount on the fund. It's not in our 'buy zone' yet, but it is remaining at a level where one can remain holding the position.

In our prior update, we discussed why a discount on EIM was more important than usual. This was because of the conditional tender offers that the fund had implemented on the back of activist investor group Karpus Management. At one point, Karpus owned just over a 20% stake, but this has come down to around 12% now.

Distribution – Coverage Remains Weak But Improving As Expected

One of the other ways activist push funds to reduce discounts is by boosting their distributions. These aren't always too long-term sustainable levels but more of trying to get a short-term reduction in the discount to exit their position more favorably. That's something EIM had done, which helps to put the current distribution rate at 6.35%.

Thanks to the fund's discount, the distribution rate on the NAV comes to 5.86%. So investors get to receive a larger distribution when CEFs trade at a discount. However, EIM's distribution coverage came in at 65% based on their last fiscal year, with the period ending September 30. In their latest semi-annual report, net investment income coverage did climb a touch to 70%. Still below the 100% level that we'd ideally like to see for a fixed-income fund.

This is where short-term interest rates come into play, as their leverage is based on floating rate notes that the Fed's target rate will impact. In the latest semi-annual report, the average interest rate edged down to 3.46% from the 4.06% previously noted.

If the Fed continues to cut, that can help to further alleviate the higher borrowing costs that EIM (and most other muni CEFs) have been experiencing. As munis pay around 3.75%, based on looking at the iShares National Muni Bond ETF's (MUB) 30-Day SEC Yield, we are now around a level that borrowings can start to boost income generation.

On the other hand, EIM still has other operating expenses, primarily the management fee. The expense ratio of 0.97% would also need to be included on top of the rate of borrowings for there to be a positive spread.

Where a lack of coverage also impacts the fund is for the tax character of the distribution. While most would be expected to be tax-free, the fund also listed almost 22% as "non-dividend distributions" (aka return of capital) for 2024. That has its own tax-friendly nature of deferring tax obligations due to reducing the cost basis for an investor, but it does change the dynamic of the characterization of the distribution overall.

EIM's Portfolio

One important factor for some investors to consider is the percentage of alternative minimum tax ("AMT") an investor could be exposed to. For EIM, they list that at just 5.72%. This is fairly minimal and consistent with the just under 5% the fund had listed previously. Another similarity with our prior update is that Texas and New York remain large weightings for the fund.

The fund lists 180 total holdings, which can provide significant diversification.

The overwhelming majority of the fund's investments are in high-credit-quality names, with only a small sliver below investment grade at 1.61%. There is also a minimal allocation to "not rated" at 5.58%. The top sectors for the fund show that general obligation, hospital, and special tax revenue are the most significant. However, they are also further diversified.

Conclusion

EIM trades at a discount that puts it near its longer-term average level. It hasn't hit our buy zone yet of a 10% discount, but with overall pressure from risk-free rates rising, it could still be an interesting time to consider adding to this position. The fund has seen its distribution coverage improve with its latest reporting as well. That is what we expected to see as the Fed cut 100 basis points toward the end of 2024. While we were expecting more cuts to follow in 2025, projections continue to push back further and for fewer. Still, any cut will bring an improvement to the fund's income generation, and we are heading in the right direction.

data.title: The U.S. Dollar Is At A Critical Inflection Point (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

The U.S. Dollar Index is at a critical inflection point, and how it behaves from here will have a major impact on the direction of gold, silver, and commodities.

As a quick reminder, the U.S. dollar typically trades inversely with commodities—when the dollar rises, commodity prices often fall, and vice versa. This is where intermarket analysis comes into play - a method of examining related markets to gain insights and anticipate moves across asset classes.

As a reminder, I track the dollar via the U.S. Dollar Index - a measure of the dollar’s exchange rate against a basket of major world currencies (not its purchasing power).

I’ve been highlighting how the U.S. Dollar Index has been teetering on the critical 100 level - a key technical support that dates back to 2023. Since then, several major dollar rallies have originated from this level, often exerting downward pressure on commodity prices - including gold and silver.

However, it’s noteworthy that the index has now broken below this key threshold, signaling an important shift in trend. If the dollar remains below 100, it would strongly indicate that further weakness lies ahead—an important bullish development for gold, silver, and the broader commodities complex.

Zooming in on the daily Dollar Index chart, it’s important to note that the index hasn’t cleanly broken down yet. Instead, it has been chopping back and forth around the key 100 level, creating a series of false moves and overall choppy, indecisive price action. However, I believe that once the dollar commits to a direction, the resulting move will be significant.

As of yesterday, the dollar is trading sharply lower - giving a lift to gold and silver - after a hit to market confidence stemming from a U.S. Court of International Trade ruling on Wednesday night. The court found that President Trump exceeded his authority in imposing “reciprocal” tariffs and ordered those measures to be vacated.

Adding to the pressure, Moody’s recent downgrade of U.S. debt has further eroded confidence in the dollar, contributing to its ongoing weakness.

As a result of yesterday's decline, the U.S. Dollar Index has once again slipped below the 100 level, signaling that another attempt to reclaim that key threshold has failed—a clear sign of weakness. If the index can finally remain below 100, there’s a strong chance it will continue to slide toward multi-year lows—a scenario that would be a major tailwind for precious metals investors. This is a critical development worth watching closely.

Although I’ve emphasized the longstanding inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and commodities, it’s especially helpful to see that correlation visually:

You can also clearly see the inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and gold:

And here is the inverse relationship between the U.S. dollar and silver:

Gold has been taking a healthy pause, trading in a consolidation range between $3,200 and $3,500 over the past month.

If the U.S. Dollar Index finally holds below the critical 100 level, it should provide the fuel gold needs to break out of that range and resume its upward trajectory—likely targeting the $4,000 level next. That’s a major technical and psychological milestone, and such round numbers often act like price magnets.

This is an exciting setup worth keeping a close eye on.

Similarly, silver has spent the past year trading beneath two major resistance zones—$32–$33 and $34–$35—even as gold has surged ahead, much to the frustration of silver bulls. However, there are numerous compelling reasons to remain bullish on silver.

If the U.S. Dollar Index continues to weaken, it may be the catalyst silver needs to finally break through those resistance levels. Once that breakout occurs, I believe silver will accelerate quickly toward $40, $50, $60, and ultimately much higher.

It’s important to recognize the high probability of a coming U.S. dollar bear market, largely due to how historically overvalued the dollar has become relative to other major currencies.

In fact, based on over 120 years of data, such extreme overvaluation has only occurred twice before—in 1933 and 1985—both of which were followed by substantial dollar declines.

The dollar’s unusual strength in recent years has been a major factor keeping commodity prices much lower than they would ordinarily be. However, an impending correction in the dollar’s value should trigger a powerful bullish surge across the commodities sector, including assets like copper, gold, silver, and mining stocks.

One of the key reasons the U.S. dollar remains so strong relative to other currencies is the massive—and unsustainable—bubble in U.S. equities, particularly in tech stocks like Nvidia and Microsoft. This has attracted a surge of foreign capital into U.S. markets, driving demand for dollars and pushing the currency much higher.

Interestingly, we saw a similar dynamic during the late 1990s dot-com bubble. When that bubble eventually burst, the dollar weakened significantly, helping to ignite the early 2000s commodities supercycle. I believe we’re on the verge of a similar pattern playing out once today’s stock market bubble finally unravels in a major way.

Numerous valuation metrics confirm that U.S. stocks - especially tech - are deep in bubble territory.

One striking example is the Nasdaq 100 relative to the U.S. M2 money supply, which shows that tech stocks have become severely overextended. The chart reveals clear parallels to the dot-com bubble, and unfortunately, I believe the outcome will be the same.

A look at the long-term U.S. Dollar Index chart reveals that it has been trading within a rising channel since around 2008. I believe an eventual breakdown from this channel would be a key signal that the dollar is entering a new bear market—much like the one that occurred in the early 2000s.

As the chart below illustrates, the euro and gold have been moving in close lockstep in recent months. If this nascent euro breakout gains traction, it should serve as a powerful catalyst for gold—potentially propelling it to $4,000 and beyond.

I believe this gold bull market is still in its early stages, with the potential to reach at least $15,000 an ounce over the next 5 to 10 years.

I previously mentioned the early-2000s commodities supercycle and why I believe another one is on the horizon—driven in large part by the U.S. dollar’s extreme overvaluation, which I expect will lead to a prolonged bear market. There’s further confirmation of this thesis in the commodities-to-Dow ratio, which shows that commodities remain extremely undervalued relative to stocks.

Historically, such imbalances don’t last; they tend to normalize over time—likely through a powerful commodities boom and a significant correction in equity markets. This aligns with my broader view that we’re in the early stages of a major capital rotation out of overvalued stocks and into still-undervalued gold and silver.

I hope you found this analysis helpful. It also serves as a great example of intermarket analysis—a method I strongly believe in and rely on heavily, both in this newsletter and in my own investing. Intermarket analysis involves examining one asset, such as the U.S. dollar, to gain insights into how correlated assets—whether positively or negatively correlated, like commodities or the euro—are likely to behave. This approach often reveals key market signals and turning points that might be missed when analyzing assets in isolation.

Intermarket analysis is a lesser-known but incredibly powerful technique. If you’d like to dive deeper into it, I highly recommend Intermarket Analysis by John Murphy—it’s one of my all-time favorite market books and an essential read for anyone serious about understanding cross-market relationships. Trust me, you won’t be disappointed.

Another book worth exploring is Intermarket Analysis and Investing by Michael E.S. Gayed, which I’ve heard great things about and recently picked up myself.

To sum up, the U.S. Dollar Index is at a critical inflection point as it hovers around the key 100 technical level. How it behaves from here will have a major impact on the direction of gold, silver, and other commodities.

My longer-term bias remains firmly to the downside, given how historically overvalued the dollar is - indicating that the path of least resistance is lower. If that scenario plays out, I expect a powerful commodities boom, with gold surging to $4,000 and silver climbing to $50+. I’ll be monitoring this closely and will keep you updated as it unfolds.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: SLV Vs. SIVR: I Prefer SIVR After U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade
data.description:
SLV and SIVR ETFs: U.S. Credit Rating Downgrade

I last analyzed the iShares Silver Trust ETF (NYSEARCA:SLV) on April 11, 2025. In that article, titled "GLD Vs. SLV: Higher Recession Probability Makes GLD More Timely," I rated SLV as a Hold after assessing the recession risks and SPDR® Gold Shares ETF's (GLD) historical role as a better defensive asset amid recessions. As for the abrdn Physical Silver Shares ETF (NYSEARCA:SIVR), I last analyzed it back in 2022 in an article titled "SIVR And GLD: Implications Of Record Dollar Strength." I also rated SIVR as a Hold after considering the strength of the dollar at that time and.

Since those publications, several materials shifts have occurred to our macroeconomic landscape including the developments of the trade conflicts, new inflation data, and most recently the downgrade of the U.S. government credit ratings by Moody's (with details quoted below for readers who need some background information).

On May 16, Moody's Ratings downgraded the U.S. credit rating, citing an inability of the nation to address large and growing deficits. This downgrade means that for the first time ever, all three major credit ratings agencies have downgraded U.S. credit below their top rating. Moody's warning comes as lawmakers are considering budget reconciliation legislation that would make America's fiscal situation significantly worse.

The net result of these developments is a sharp decline of the dollar index since my last writings as you can see from the following chart. In particular, the ICE U.S. Dollar Index (DXY) was around or above 110 when I last covered SLV and SIVR. The above macroscopic changes have caused DXY to decrease to the current level of 99.3 as seen, close to the historical average of 98.4 observed in the past decade.

These changes call for a reexamination of my silver thesis for the longer term. And my key thoughts from this examination are twofold. First, I believe the U.S. credit downgrade will generate long-term impact on US dollars. As a result, as long as silver price is quoted in U.S. dollars, I feel more bullish about both SLV and SIVR than I did before. Second, since I expect the impacts to be long-term oriented, I am looking for an ETF that I can buy and hold, rather than trade. As such, I prefer SIVR over SLV due to its lower expense ratios. With these considerations, this follow-up article upgrades my rating on SIVR to buy, and reiterates my hold rating on SLV as further elaborated below.

SIVR and SLV ETFs: Basic Facts and Competing Funds

Before diving into further details, let me cover some basics of both the SIVR and SLV ETFs for investors new to them. SIVR and SLV ETF both use physical silver bullions stored in secured vaults to track the spot price of silver as detailed in their fund description below.

SLV Description: SLV tracks the silver spot price, less expenses and liabilities, using silver bullion held in London. SLV gives investors direct exposure to silver by physically holding the metal in vaults in London.

SIVR Description: SIVR tracks the silver spot price, less trust expenses, using silver bullion. SIVR gives investors pure exposure to silver by holding the physical metal in trust. Each share represents a fractional interest in the trust. The metal is held with JPMorgan Chase Bank in London at a secured vault. Thus, providing a convenient way to invest in silver.

Investors have a few choices in the silver space as listed below. The SLV fund is one of the most prominent and largest funds here. The fund was incepted in April 2006 and manages a substantial AUM of $15.34 billion, far ahead of its competing funds. For example, SIVR was incepted a few years later in 2009 and now has an AUM of $1.70 billion only. Other more recent funds include the Sprott Physical Silver Trust (PSLV), which was launched in 2010 and has $6.10 billion in AUM. Lastly, the UBS ETRACS Silver Shares Covered Call ETN (SLVO), with an inception date in 2013, has a relatively small AUM at $191.27 million.

As such, SLV certainly offers the best liquidity and tradability for investors who trade more often. But for investors like myself who don't, SIVR offers an optimal combination of liquidity and fees in my view. It charges an expense ratio of 0.30%, the lowest among the funds listed here.

SIVR and SLV ETFs: Correlation with Dollar Index

With the above background, let's return to the potential impact of the U.S. credit downgrade. As aforementioned, I expect the impacts to be gradual and long-lasting. As a historical context, the chart below shows how rare such downgrades are. As seen, Moody's has maintained the highest rating for the U.S. government throughout the historical drama in the country's history (and even the two World Wars did not trigger a downgrade).

However, once downgraded, I could see several potential impacts to gradually unfold in the years to come such as the reduced demand for U.S. dollar-based assets as other countries seek to diversify their reserve, and also higher borrowing costs for the U.S. government (I will revisit this a bit later). These potential risks are further compounded by the U.S.'s current budget reconciliation uncertainties. I expect these impacts to keep their pressure on the dollar index. As aforementioned, I expect a decline in dollar index to support high silver prices as illustrated by the following historical data.

To wit, the top panel of the following chart below illustrates the correlation between SLV price and DXY level in the past 10 years since 2015, and the bottom panel illustrates the correlation between SIVR Price and DXY. Both charts show a strong negative correlation, with the current correlation for SLV being -0.624 and for SIVR being -0.628. Over the long term, the average correlation for both funds against the dollar index have been significantly negative at -0.391. Also, note the correlation has hovered around and/or below a level of -0.9 (and reached as far as -0.959) for extended periods of times. A correlation of -1 means a perfect inverse correlation.

Other Risks and Final Thoughts

Both SIVR and SLV entail the same set of risks generic to most commodity funds. The top ones on my list are the so-called counterparty risk and also tax headwinds. Counterparty risk stems from the fact that investors of SIVR and SLV do not directly own the physical silver bars. Therefore, if the custodian of the funds were to face financial difficulties or fail to adequately fulfill its responsibilities, investors could face a downside risk. Also, gains on both funds are taxed as gains from collectible, whose tax rates are higher than those on capital gains for most investors.

To revisit a comment made earlier, I mentioned that the downgraded credit rating of the U.S. government could cause its borrowing cost to increase in the future. As a specific example, immediately after the downgrade was announced, the 10-Year Treasury Rate (US10Y) began to show upward movements, as you can see from the following chart. The rates have pushed above 4.5% and currently hover around 4.58%. The rise in longer-term rates is even more pronounced, with 30-year treasury rates surpassing 5% shortly after the downgrade.

My forgoing argument focuses on explaining why this could be a positive catalysts for silver (XAGUSD:CUR). However, my argument will be one-sided if I don't point out the fact that higher treasury rates also make treasury bonds a more attractive asset class, especially when compared to commodity funds that don't provide active income (and charge a relative high fee when compared to large bond or equity funds).

To close, recent changes in our macroscopic parameters have promoted me to rethink my thesis on SIVR and SLV. My key takeaways are twofold. Firstly, I expect the U.S. credit downgrade to have gradual but long-lasting impacts on the U.S. dollar and silver pricing. Secondly, and consequently, I feel more positive about both SIVR and SLV than at the times of my last writings. With my long-term orientation, I like SIVR better, as it presents a more optimal balance between fees and liquidity. For these considerations, this article adjusts my rating on SIVR to buy, and we do hold a sizable SIVR position in our account you can see from the following updates we recently provided to our Investing Group (Envision Early Retirement).

data.title: Kingsoft Cloud: Enterprise Cloud Drag, Margin Pressure, And Geopolitical Risk Keep Stock On Hold
data.description:
Kingsoft Cloud (NASDAQ:KC) reported Q1 2025 with revenue and adjusted EBITDA that came in below consensus due to a miss on the enterprise cloud that was negatively impacted by a combination of factors, including Chinese New Year and global supply chain uncertainties. The increase in AI investment also negatively impacted margins.

We are neutral on KC and rate the stock as a HOLD given the limited upside to valuation. We value the stock at 2.5x forward revenue multiple, in line with comparables.

We would be more bullish on the stock if there is greater visibility into enterprise demand, as well as limited competition from DeepSeek. We note that management observed some demand pullback from independent large language model companies due to the success of DeepSeek, and we view this as a minor negative, as demand could shift to the larger cloud providers, such as Alibaba. We recommend investors stay on the sidelines for a better entry point.

Underwhelming Headline Due to Macro Overhang

Total revenue grew 11% y/y to RMB 1.97bn, trailing consensus by roughly 10%. Public cloud sales grew 14% y/y to RMB 1.35bn, with AI gross billings increasing by 200% y/y to RMB 525mn, accounting for 39% of public cloud revenue.

The weakness was on the enterprise side, where revenue grew only 5% year-over-year to RMB 616.5mn. Consistent with our coverage of GDS and VNET, China’s enterprise cloud segment is seeing some headwinds due to the seasonality of the Chinese New Year and intense competition with Alibaba, Tencent, and Baidu ramping up on their cloud offerings. Although KC has made strides in AI-related public cloud services, the overall growth in public cloud revenue still faces headwinds from pricing pressures and customer acquisition costs. In terms of revenue contribution, Xiaomi and Kingsoft Ecosystem revenue increased by 50% y/y to RMB 500mn, accounting for 25% of total revenue.

Management is optimistic about Xiaomi’s AI, as are we (see:Xiaomi: From Silicon To SUVs, Driving The Next Era Of Smart, Affordable Tech). However, we note that Xiaomi AI is still in the early stage of growth and will require substantial time and investment to scale. The ramp-up period involves product development, market adoption, and integration with Kingsoft Cloud’s infrastructure, which implies that the immediate revenue impact is likely to be limited. This delayed monetization adds to the short-term uncertainty in Kingsoft Cloud’s revenue trajectory, in our view.

All-in-One Machine Remains a Proof of Concept

KC has introduced an all-in-one machine solution designed to offer a bundled hardware-software infrastructure optimized for AI workloads. This product leverages advanced Intel Xeon Scalable processors and includes pre-optimized AI frameworks such as Intel Optimization for TensorFlow and Caffe, that enhance AI development by reducing the need for customers to fine-tune AI software stacks themselves.

We believe there is a market for this device, particularly among SMEs with limited IT budgets; however, we foresee a few adoption hurdles in the near term. First, enterprise customers often have established infrastructure preferences and may be hesitant to switch to a new integrated solution without proven large-scale deployments. The all-in-one concept may not be suitable for the majority of customers, as most enterprise use cases require a certain degree of customization.

Second, migration cost may be an issue, as the upfront investment and migration could drive up the total cost of ownership for this service. This may not appear to be attractive when competing cloud providers offer more flexible pay-as-you-go models that do not put a strain on the company’s capex budget.

Finally, customer education will likely take some time, and we question whether time is on KC's or any other cloud provider’s side, given the competitive environment in China. Given that many end-users want to see clear performance and cost benefits in real-world applications before a sizable commitment, we wonder how fast this ramp-up could be.

Potential Negative Impact of the H20 AI Ban

During the call, management pointed out that the risk of an H20 ban is limited in the near term, while acknowledging the need to transition to domestic chips in the mid- to long-term. We note that KC has been working with domestic chip manufacturers for the past two years and uses domestic chips in its own model training.

We believe the growing Sino-U.S. tension on technology curtailment will be a negative for KC, given that the H20 chip has been one of the most advanced AI GPUs for the cloud providers on training and inference for large AI workloads. Switching to domestic chips may result in a satisfactory outcome, but when compared with other cloud providers with large H20 stockpiles, we question whether KC could continue to deliver a competitive cloud service.

Mixed Bag With Leasing

During the call, management stated that AI infrastructure will be partially financed by leasing third-party servers, thereby reducing the burden of paying out of pocket from the company’s own cash. The company has negotiated a setup with third-party stock from Q1. Management pointed out that they will give investors a full picture of the breakdown between cash capex and leasing starting next quarter.

The plus side of this capex decision is that KC can reserve its own cash amid an uncertain macro backdrop in China, where cloud demand could be unpredictable. The minus side of this would be gross margin pressure on KC. Note that when KC leases assets such as servers for its cloud business, it has to pay periodic lease fees, and these fees are ongoing expenses that reduce the gross margin on the revenue generated from using these leased assets. This is different from owning the assets outright, which involves one-time cash capex that can be depreciated over time. As such, we expect leasing could add to KC’s margin pressure in the near term and negatively impact the earnings outlook for the rest of the year.

In short, we remain neutral on KC and await a more favorable entry point for the stock, given that China’s soft macroeconomic environment may continue to dampen demand for cloud services, and that near-term margin pressure may be unavoidable due to the shift to leasing. The long-term impact of geopolitical risk also paints a precarious picture of the outlook for this stock. We recommend investors stay on the sidelines and stick with a proven cloud provider such as Alibaba (see: Alibaba: Pullback Sets Up Compelling Long-Term Opportunity (Rating Upgrade))

data.title: Weitz Short Duration Income Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
The Short Duration Income Fund’s Institutional Class returned +1.62% in the first quarter compared to a +1.63% return for the Bloomberg U.S. Aggregate 1-3 Year Index.

While any quarterly commentary will be stale by the time it is published, the first quarter of 2025 might be a particular case in point after the market turbulence brought about by the ‘Liberation Day’ tariff news on April 2, 2025. For a post-quarter commentary of the market reaction to the news of early April, read the first quarter Fixed Income Insight letter titled “Tariff Tantrum.”

Now, on to the first quarter

Interest rates, particularly shorter-term, declined during the first quarter of 2025 despite no interest rate moves by the Federal Reserve. 2-, 3-, and 5-year Treasury rates (the part of the yield curve that most impacts the Short Duration Income Fund) peaked in early January. Then they proceeded to decline from that peak to quarter-end by one-quarter of one percent (5-year Treasuries) to as much as four-tenths of one percent (3-year Treasuries). See below for our standard view of the U.S. Treasury yield curve at three different times (quarter-end, year-end, and one year before quarter-end).

As measured by credit spreads, risk assets widened (increased) during the quarter. Broad investment-grade spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) increased by 15 basis points (bps) from 82 to 97 bps. High yield spreads (as measured by the ICE BofA) widened (increased) 61 basis points from 292 to 355 bps. After many years of declines, the quarter’s higher credit spreads were (and are) a welcome change. Higher credit spreads imply a potential improvement in the risk/reward environment for deploying capital in the Short Duration Income Fund.

Portfolio Positioning

The table on the next page shows the change in allocation to various sectors, from the prior quarter and from the prior year. This summary provides a view over time of how we have allocated capital. Since our goal is to invest in sectors that we believe offer the best risk-adjusted returns, our allocations may change significantly over time.
